Algorithm eﬃciency analysis
M˘ad˘alina R˘aschip, Cristian Gat¸u
Faculty of Computer Science
“Alexandru Ioan Cuza” University of Ia¸si, Romania
DS 2018/2019

Content
Algorithm eﬃciency analysis
Computation examples
Growth order
Asymptotic notation
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
2 / 52

Execution time
“As soon as an Analytic Engine exists, it will necessarily guide the
future course of the science. Whenever any result is sought by its
aid, the question will arise—By what course of calculation can
these results be arrived at by the machine in the shortest time?”
Charles Babbage (1864)
Analytic machine
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
3 / 52

Challenge
For high dimension input data, the algorithm will still solve the problem?
Knuth (1970): Scientiﬁc methods should be used in order to understand
the algorithm performance.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
4 / 52

Algorithm eﬃciency analysis
(cid:73) Complexity analysis.
(cid:73) Estimation of the computing resources volume required to execute
the algorithm:
(cid:73) memory space: data storage required space;
(cid:73) execution time: algorithm execution time.
(cid:73) Eﬃcient algorithm: requires a reasonable amount of computing
resources:
(cid:73) eﬃciency is measured with respect to the memory space or execution
time;
(cid:73) Utility:
(cid:73) to establish the algorithm performance and supply guarantees on this
performance;
(cid:73) to compare algorithms.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
5 / 52

Time eﬃciency analysis
“It is convenient to have a measure of the amount of work
involved in a computing process, even though it be a very crude
one. We may count up the number of times that various
elementary operations are applied in the whole process and then
given them various weights. We might, for instance, count the
number of additions, subtractions, multiplications, divisions,
recording of numbers, and extractions of ﬁgures from tables. In
the case of computing with matrices most of the work consists of
multiplications and writing down numbers, and we shall therefore
only attempt to count the number of multiplications and
recordings.”
— Alan Turing, Bounding-oﬀ errors in matrix processes, 1947
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
6 / 52

Time eﬃciency analysis
We need to set
I. A computing model.
von Neumann model – RAM (random access machine):
(cid:73) the processing are executed sequentially;
(cid:73) memory is an inﬁnite set of cells;
(cid:73) the time to access any data is the same (does not depend on the
memory location);
(cid:73) the memory cells store “small” values (their dimension is polynomial
bounded);
(cid:73) the execution time of one processing step does not depend on the
operand values.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
7 / 52

Time eﬃciency analysis
II. A unit for the execution time
(cid:73) Pseudo-cod (lecture 1):
(cid:73) variables and elementary data types; instructions; procedures and
functions.
(cid:73) Execution time of one elementary processing:
(cid:73) elementary operations: assignment, arithmetical operations,
comparisons, logical operations;
(cid:73) each elementary operation requires a single time unit for its execution.
(cid:73) The total execution time equals the number of executed elementary
operations.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
9 / 52

Problem size
(cid:73) Assumption: the computing resources volume depends on the input
data volume.
(cid:73) Problem size: memory volume required to store the input data.
(cid:73) Can be expressed as:
(cid:73) number of input data components or
(cid:73) number of bits required to store the input data.
(cid:73) Number of bits required to store the value n is [log2n] + 1.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
10 / 52

Problem size: examples
(cid:73) Test whether a number n is prime:
n ( or log2n ).
(cid:73) Finding the minimum of an array: x[0..n − 1]:
n.
(cid:73) Addition of two matrices (m × n): m × n.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
11 / 52

Function product(a[0..m − 1, 0..n − 1], b[0..n − 1, 0..p − 1])
begin
1
2

4
5
6
for i ← 0 to m − 1 do
for j ← 0 to p − 1 do
c[i, j] ← 0
for k ← 0 to n − 1 do
c[i, j] ← c[i, j] + a[i, k] ∗ b[k, j]
return c[0..m − 1, 0..p − 1]
end

Example 3. Minimum of an array
Execution time depends on:
(cid:73) problem size;
(cid:73) input data properties.
Extreme cases have to be considered:
(cid:73) most favorable case
(cid:73) x[0] <= x[i], i = 0, . . . , n − 1 ⇒ t(n) = 0 ⇒ T (n) = 3n
(cid:73) worst-case
(cid:73) x[0] > x[1] > ... > x[n − 1] ⇒ t(n) = n − 1 ⇒ T (n) = 4n − 1
(cid:73) 3n <= T (n) <= 4n − 1
Both the lower and the upper bound depends linearly on the problem
size.
(cid:73) If only the basic operation (comparison x[i] < m) is counted then:
T (n) = n − 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019

for j ← 1 to n − 1 do
while i >= 0 and a[i] > key do
key ← a[j]
i ← j − 1
a[i + 1] ← a[i]
i ← i − 1
a[i + 1] ← key

Algorithm analysis
(cid:73) The most favorable case
(cid:73) A lower bound for the running time;
(cid:73) Identify ineﬃcient algorithms:
(cid:73) if an algorithm has a high cost in the most favorable case then it might
be not an acceptable solution.
(cid:73) Worst-case
(cid:73) The highest running time with respect to all possible input date;
(cid:73) An upper-bound for the running time;
(cid:73) The upper-bound is more important than the lower bound.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
25 / 52

Average-case analysis
(cid:73) There are situations where both the most favorable and the
worst-case are exceptions:
(cid:73) these cases analysis does not provide enough information.
(cid:73) Average-case analysis aims at providing information on the
algorithm behavior on arbitrary input data.
(cid:73) It is based on the knowledge of the input data probability
distribution.
(cid:73) Knowledge (estimation) of the outcome probability of each possible
input instance.
(cid:73) Average running time is the average of running times corresponding
to all input instances.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
26 / 52

Average-case analysis
(cid:73) Assumptions on the input data distribution probability:
(cid:73) input instances can be grouped in classes (the running time is the same
for instances of same class);
(cid:73) there are m classes of input instances;
(cid:73) the outcome probability of an instance from k class is Pk ;
(cid:73) the running time for a instance from k class is Tk (n).
(cid:73) The average running time:
Ta(n) = P1T1(n) + P2T2(n) + ... + PmTm(n)
(cid:73) If all classes have the same outcome probability:
Ta(n) = (T1(n) + T2(n) + ... + Tm(n))/m
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
27 / 52

Example 4. Sequential search (re-visited)
(cid:73) Assumptions:
(cid:73) probability that v belongs to the array is: p
(cid:73) v can be found with same probability on any position of the array;
(cid:73) probability that v is found on the k-th position: p/n;
(cid:73) probability that v is not found in the array: 1 − p.
(cid:73) Ta(n) = p(1+2+..+n)
n
+ (1 − p)n = p(n+1)
2 + (1 − p)n = (1 − p
2 )n + p
2
(cid:73) if p = 0.5, then Ta(n) = 3
(cid:73) the average time depends linearly on the input data size.
4 n + 1
4 ;
(cid:73) Remark: the average time is not necessarily the arithmetic mean of
the extreme case running times.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
28 / 52

Algorithm analysis steps
1. Problem size identiﬁcation.
2. Dominant operation identiﬁcation.
3. Running time estimation (dominant operation running times).
4. if the running time depends on the input data properties then the
following cased are analyzed:
(cid:73) most favorable case;
(cid:73) worst-case;
(cid:73) average case.
5. The complexity order (class) is established.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
29 / 52

Algorithm eﬃciency analysis
(cid:73) Aim: ﬁnd the impact on the running time of the problem size
increase.
(cid:73) The detailed expression of the running time is not required.
(cid:73) Is is enough to identify:
(cid:73) Running time growth order;
(cid:73) Eﬃciency class (complexity) of the algorithm.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
30 / 52

Growth order
(cid:73) Dominant term: the term that becomes signiﬁcantly higher when
the problem size increases.
(cid:73) Dictates the algorithm behavior when the problem size increases.
Running time
Dominant term
T 1(n) = an + b
T 2(n) = alogn + b
T 3(n) = an2 + bn + c
T 4(n) = an + bn + c

Growth orders
In order to compare the growth orders of two running times T 1(n) and
T 2(n), the limn→∞
T 1(n)
T 2(n) is computed.
(cid:73) if lim = 0: T 1(n) has a smaller growth order than T 2(n);
(cid:73) if lim = c, c > 0 constant: T 1(n) and T 2(n) have the same growth
order;
(cid:73) if lim = ∞: T 1(n) has a bigger growth order than T 2(n).

Asymptotic analysis
(cid:73) The analysis of running times for small values of problem size does
not allow to identify the eﬃcient and ineﬃcient algorithms.
(cid:73) Growth order diﬀerences becomes more signiﬁcant when the problem
size increases.
(cid:73) Asymptotic analysis: studies the running time properties when the
problem size grows to inﬁnity (big size problems).
(cid:73) algorithms can be classiﬁed using notations: O, Ω, Θ
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
38 / 52

Asymptotic growth order. O notation
Let f , g : N → R+ two functions of problem size.
Deﬁnition
O(g (n)) = {f (n) : ∃c > 0, ∃n0 ∈ N a.ˆı. ∀n >= n0, 0 <= f (n) <= cg (n)}.
Notation:
f (n) = O(g (n))
(f (n) has a growth order at most equal to the one of g (n).)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
39 / 52

O notation
For big enough values of n, f (n) is upper bounded by g (n) multiplied by a
positive constant.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
40 / 52

O notations - proprieties
1. f (n) ∈ O(f (n)) (reﬂexive).
2. f (n) ∈ O(g (n)), g (n) ∈ O(h(n)) ⇒ f (n) ∈ O(h(n)) (transitive).
3. If T (n) = ad nd + ad−1nd−1 + ... + a1n + a0 then T (n) ∈ O(nk ) for
any k >= d.
(cid:73) example: n ∈ O(n2)
4. If for the worst case T (n) <= g (n), then T (n) ∈ O(g (n)).
(cid:73) Sequential search: 5 ≤ T (n) ≤ 3n + 2 ⇒ the algorithm belongs to
O(n) class.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
42 / 52

Ω notation
Deﬁnition
Ω(g (n)) = {f (n): ∃c > 0, n0 ∈ N s.t. ∀n >= n0 : f (n) ≥ cg (n)}
Notation:
f (n) = Ω(g (n))
(f (n) has a growth order at least as big as the one of g (n).)
Examples:
1. T (n) = 3n + 3 ⇒ T (n) ∈ Ω(n)
3n <= 3n + 3, c = 3, n0 = 1, g (n) = n
2. 5 <= T (n) <= 3n + 2 ⇒ T (n) ∈ Ω(1)
c = 5, n0 = 1, g (n) = 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
43 / 52

Ω notation
For big values of n, the f (n) function is lower bounded by g (n) multiplied
by a positive constant.
FII, UAIC
DS 2018/2019
Lecture 2
44 / 52

Ω notation – properties
1. f (n) ∈ Ω(f (n)) (reﬂexive).
2. f (n) ∈ Ω(g (n)), g (n) ∈ Ω(h(n)) ⇒ f (n) ∈ Ω(h(n)) (transitive).
3. If T (n) = ad nd + ad−1nd−1 + ... + a1n + a0 then T (n) ∈ Ω(nk ) for
any k <= d.
(cid:73) example: n2 ∈ Ω(n)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
45 / 52

Θ notation
Deﬁnition
Θ(g (n)) =
{f (n) : ∃c1, c2 > 0, n0 ∈ N a.ˆı. ∀n ≥ n0 : c1g (n) ≤ f (n) ≤ c2g (n)}.
Notation:
f (n) = Θ(g (n))
(f (n) has the same growth order as g (n).)
Examples:
1. T (n) = 3n + 3 ⇒ T (n) ∈ Θ(n)
c1 = 2, c2 = 4, n0 = 3, g (n) = n
2. Finding minimum of an array:
3n <= T (n) <= 4n − 1 ⇒ T (n) ∈ Θ(n)
c1 = 3, c2 = 4, n0 = 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
46 / 52

Θ notation
For big enough values of n, f (n) is both lower and upper bounded by g (n)
multiplied by some positive constants.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
47 / 52

Θ notations – properties
1. f (n) ∈ Θ(f (n)) (reﬂexive).
2. f (n) ∈ Θ(g (n)), g (n) ∈ Θ(h(n)) ⇒ f (n) ∈ Θ(h(n)) (transitive).
3. f (n) ∈ Θ(g (n)) ⇒ g (n) ∈ Θ(f (n)) (symmetric).
4. If T (n) = ad nd + ad−1nd−1 + ... + a1n + a0 then T (n) ∈ Θ(nd ).
5. Θ(cg (n)) = Θ(g (n)) for any constant c.
Special cases:
(cid:73) Θ(c) = Θ(1)
(cid:73) Θ(logah(n)) = Θ(logbh(n)) for any a, b > 1
6. Θ(f (n) + g (n)) = Θ(max{f (n), g (n)})
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
48 / 52

Θ notations – properties
7. Θ(g (n)) ⊂ O(g (n)).
Example: f (n) = 10n lg n + 5,
f (n) ≤ g (n) for any n ≥ 36 ⇒ f (n) ∈ O(g (n))
But there are not such constants c and n0 such that
cn2 ≤ 10nlgn + 5 for any n ≥ n0.
g (n) = n2
8. Θ(g (n)) ⊂ Ω(g (n)).
Example: f (n) = 10nlgn + 5,
f (n) ≥ 10g (n) for any n ≥ 1 ⇒ f (n) ∈ Ω(g (n))
But there are not such constants c and n0 such that 10nlgn + 5 ≤ cn
for any n ≥ n0.
g (n) = n
9. Θ(g (n)) = O(g (n)) ∩ Ω(g (n)).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
49 / 52

Θ notation – examples
1. Multiplication of two matrices: T (m, n, p) = 4mnp + 5mp + 4m + 2.
Deﬁnition extension to the case where the problem size depends on
more values:
f (m, n, p) ∈ Θ(g (m, n, p)) if it exists c1, c2 > 0 and m0, n0, p0 ∈ N
such that c1g (m, n, p) ≤ f (m, n, p) ≤ c2g (m, n, p) for any
m ≥ m0, n ≥ n0, p ≥ p0.
Thus T (m, n, p) ∈ Θ(mnp).
2. Sequential search: 5 ≤ T (n) ≤ 3n + 2.
If T (n) = 5 then it does not exists c1 such that 5 ≥ c1n for big
enough values of n ⇒ T (n) does not belong to Θ(n).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 2
DS 2018/2019
50 / 52

Θ notation – examples
When n is large enough, an algorithm of Θ(n2) complexity is more eﬃcient
than one of Θ(n3) complexity.
